success_surface: Row filtering based on cell content patterns with diagnostic validation
effective_behaviors: Exploratory data inspection before transformation to understand schema and edge cases
Inverse operation strategy: keep matching rows instead of deleting non-matching rows
Comprehensive logging of before/after row counts and sample verification data
Pattern matching using str.startswith() for multi-value prefix filtering
Explicit column reference by position when header names are uncertain
evidence: Agent printed column names, sample values, and unique first characters before filtering
Used df.iloc[:, 1] to access target column without relying solely on headers
Applied astype(str).str.startswith(('H', 'A')) for clean multi-pattern matching
Logged original count (10410), filtered count (0), and unique first characters post-filter
Provided dual solution covering both Python implementation and original VBA context

memory_items.title: Data Exploration Before Transformation
memory_items.lesson: Always inspect workbook schema, column positions, and data patterns before applying modifications
memory_items.generalization: When the instruction does not specify exact column indices, verify the worksheet structure by printing headers and sampling values. Check for edge cases like empty cells, mixed types, or unexpected patterns.

memory_items.title: Keep-Matching Instead of Delete-Nonmatching
memory_items.lesson: Filter to retain valid rows rather than iterate and delete invalid ones for better performance and safety
memory_items.generalization: When asked to remove rows meeting certain criteria, construct a filter that keeps rows NOT meeting those criteria. Use boolean indexing with negative conditions or invert the logic explicitly.

memory_items.title: String Pattern Matching Best Practices
memory_items.lesson: Use pandas string methods with type coercion for reliable prefix/suffix matching across mixed data types
memory_items.generalization: For text pattern operations, convert columns to string first using astype(str), then apply str.startswith(), str.endswith(), or str.contains(). Handle NaN values with fillna() if needed.

memory_items.title: Diagnostic Logging for Verification
memory_items.lesson: Log before/after metrics and sample outputs to validate transformations
memory_items.generalization: Always print row counts before and after filtering, show unique values of affected columns post-transformation, and optionally display sample rows to confirm the filter produced expected results.

edits.content:
## Success Patterns from Trajectory Analysis

### Row Filtering Strategy
- Prefer boolean indexing to **keep matching rows** rather than iterating to delete non-matching rows (safer, faster)
- When filtering text patterns, use: `df[col].astype(str).str.startswith((value1, value2))`
- Log before/after row counts and unique values to validate filter correctness

### Column Reference Robustness
- When headers may be missing or ambiguous, reference columns by position (`iloc`) while verifying via exploratory prints
- Print sample values and column metadata before major transformations

### Diagnostic Validation
- Always include print statements showing: original row count, filtered row count, and unique values in affected column(s)
- Display sample rows post-filter to visually confirm expected results

### Edge Case Handling
- Convert columns to string before pattern matching (`astype(str)`) to handle numeric or mixed-type data
- Use `fillna('')` if cells may contain NaN before checking prefixes/suffixes
